Safety Issues.finding: Exterior painted wood surfaces show widespread paint failure with flaking and peeling, creating a potential lead-based paint exposure hazard due to the home’s age.
The step from the rear deck to the yard is excessively high and presents a tripping hazard during normal use.
The occupant door between the garage and the interior living space is not fire rated, reducing required fire separation protection.
PEX water supply lines are installed within close proximity to the water heater flue, creating a potential heat damage and safety concern.
The toilet at the owner’s bathroom is loose at the floor and not securely mounted.
Service entrance conductors run through or in close proximity to tree limbs, creating a risk of damage and electrical hazard.
One exterior electrical outlet tested ungrounded and did not trip when tested, indicating a shock hazard.
The furnace flue is missing required fasteners at multiple joints, increasing the risk of exhaust gas leakage.
Fireplace firebrick grout is loose, missing, or deteriorated within the burn area, reducing fire containment safety.

Infestation.finding: Cut firewood is stored directly against the exterior wall, which can attract wood-destroying insects and other pests.
Animal feces are present in multiple areas of the attic, indicating current or past pest activity.

Evaluate.finding: Brick veneer lacks visible drainage or weep holes in several areas, limiting proper moisture drainage and requiring further evaluation by a qualified contractor.
One or more decks are installed close to grade or enclosed with skirting, preventing full inspection of deck structure and underlying conditions.
Unknown biological growth is present on the front soffit, and further evaluation is needed to determine moisture source and potential health impact.
Interior floors slope in multiple areas of the home, requiring further evaluation to determine cause and structural significance.
Signs of rafter spread are visible along the attic ridge in several locations and require further structural evaluation.
Multiple plumbing waste lines in the crawlspace have improper slope and require further evaluation for proper drainage.
Multiple deficiencies are present inside the electrical panel, including sharp cover screws and missing connectors, requiring licensed electrician evaluation.
Branch circuit wiring deficiencies are present, including missing junction boxes and improperly covered connections, requiring further evaluation.
Attic insulation is light in most areas and requires further evaluation to determine adequacy and coverage.
The crawlspace vapor barrier is missing in multiple areas, requiring further evaluation due to potential moisture migration concerns.
Bathroom exhaust ducts terminate in the attic instead of venting to the exterior, requiring further evaluation for moisture accumulation risk.

Minor Concerns.finding: Drip edge flashing is not installed in multiple roof areas, indicating improper roof edge protection and incomplete flashing installation.
At least one plumbing vent does not meet required clearance height standards for the area and is improperly terminated through the roof.
Brick veneer is in direct contact with soil or landscaping in multiple areas around the exterior perimeter.
Brick veneer exhibits light cracking in multiple locations, indicating minor masonry movement or mortar deterioration.
The front concrete porch slab shows light cracking in at least one area.
A concrete pad at the rear entry garage door is cracked and shows signs of surface deterioration.
The asphalt driveway contains multiple small cracks and areas of surface deterioration.
The rear yard grading slopes toward the home, preventing proper drainage away from the foundation.
One rake board at the rear of the home shows visible deterioration and material damage.
Moisture staining is visible on the rear soffit, indicating prior moisture exposure at this location.
The garage ceiling does not provide proper air separation between the garage and attic space.
The garage floor contains multiple small cracks across the concrete surface.
The rear garage entry door allows visible daylight when closed, indicating poor sealing or alignment.
Grout is cracked or deteriorated in areas of the hall bathroom tiled wall surfaces.
Flooring materials show delamination and separation in multiple areas throughout the home.
Silicone caulk is missing or insufficient at the shower floor and threshold in the owner’s bathroom.
Bedroom and bathroom doors are missing door stops or installed stops do not function properly.
One set of bedroom closet doors does not close completely due to misalignment or hardware issues.
A visible gap exists between the laundry room window frame and sill.
An added support post in the crawlspace appears temporary and is not installed as a permanent structural support.
Moisture staining is visible on attic framing members in multiple locations.
The exterior hose bib at the front of the home is missing an operating handle and cannot be used as intended.
Hot water flow at the kitchen sink is reversed, indicating improper faucet orientation or connection.
The tub spout and control handle at the hall bathroom are loose at the wall surface.
Several plumbing supply lines in the crawlspace are connected using mixed materials and fittings.
Temporary repairs are present on multiple plumbing waste lines in the crawlspace.
The water heater flue is missing required fasteners at multiple joints.
Several three-prong electrical outlets are loose within the wall at various interior locations.
No recent service documentation or labels were observed on the HVAC system components.
One handle on the living room fireplace doors is loose and does not operate securely.
The fireplace damper lid is missing or not installed in the living room fireplace.
The exterior air conditioning condenser unit is dirty and obstructed by debris.
A whole-house attic fan does not appear operable and lacks a clearly identified control switch.

Prepare.finding: Furnishings and stored household items limited visibility in several areas and may have restricted access to components during the inspection.
Gutters contain accumulated debris in multiple sections, preventing full visual evaluation of gutter condition and drainage performance.
Exterior light fixtures are not sealed to the wall surface, allowing potential moisture entry behind the fixtures.
The sill plate and rim joist were not visible due to insulation coverage, limiting inspection of these components.
Additional detached structures on the property were not inspected and were excluded from the inspection scope.
The water heater is old and at or beyond its expected service life.
The main water shut-off valve location could not be identified during the inspection.
The main fuel shut-off valve is located at the exterior gas meter.
The main electrical panel and one subpanel are located within the garage area.
Smoke detectors and available carbon monoxide detectors were tested using the test buttons during the inspection.
Smoke detectors should be tested at common hallway locations after moving into the home.
Cooling performance temperature differential testing was not performed due to low exterior air temperatures.
Dryer vent cleaning is recommended periodically to maintain proper airflow and reduce lint accumulation risk.
